title: PySpark Test Track
description: Test track with 3 sample modules: Junior DataFrames, Mid-Level Join Performance, Senior Partition Strategy.

chapters.title: DataFrames — The Core Abstraction
chapters.description: Understand DataFrames as Spark's primary data structure, master creation patterns, learn the immutability model, and build the habit of inspecting schemas before transforming data.

blocks.content: A DataFrame is a distributed collection of rows organized into named columns. If you have used a spreadsheet, a SQL table, or a Pandas DataFrame, you already understand the shape. The difference is where it lives: a Spark DataFrame is not stored on one machine. It is split across many machines, and every operation you write runs in parallel across all of them.
The Mental Model

Think of a DataFrame as a table blueprint that spans a cluster. When you write df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 1000), you are not scanning rows on your laptop. You are sending an instruction to dozens of executors, each holding a partition of the data, and they all apply the filter simultaneously.

blocks.title: DataFrame across a cluster
blocks.content:
DataFrame: meter_readings

  Logical View (what you see):
  | meter_id | daily_kw | date     | region   |
  | M-001    | 1,250    | 2026-01  | NORTH    |
  | M-002    | 890      | 2026-01  | SOUTH    |

  Physical Reality:
  Executor 1: Partition 0 (170K rows)
  Executor 2: Partition 1 (170K rows)
  Executor 3: Partition 2 (160K rows)
blocks.caption: You see one table. Spark sees partitions spread across executors.

blocks.content: DataFrame vs RDD

Spark has an older API called the RDD. RDDs are low-level — you work with raw Python objects and Spark cannot optimize your code. DataFrames are high-level — you describe columns and transformations, and Spark's Catalyst optimizer figures out the fastest way to execute. In 2026, there is almost no reason to use RDDs directly.

blocks.title: Key insight
blocks.content: A DataFrame is not data. It is a plan for computing data. Until you call an action (show, count, write), nothing executes. This laziness is what lets Spark optimize your entire transformation chain before running it.
Most DataFrames come from reading files — CSV, Parquet, JSON. But sometimes you need a small DataFrame created from scratch: a reference lookup, a configuration table, a set of test data.
From a List of Tuples

from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid').getOrCreate()

regions = [
    ('NORTH', 'Nordic Region', 125000),
    ('SOUTH', 'Southern Grid', 140000),
    ('EAST',  'Eastern Zone',  110000),
    ('WEST',  'Western Block',  95000),
]

df = spark.createDataFrame(regions, ['region_code', 'region_name', 'meter_count'])
df.show()
From a List of Dictionaries

regions = [
    {'region_code': 'NORTH', 'region_name': 'Nordic Region', 'meter_count': 125000},
    {'region_code': 'SOUTH', 'region_name': 'Southern Grid', 'meter_count': 140000},
]
df = spark.createDataFrame(regions)
From a Pandas DataFrame

import pandas as pd
pd_df = pd.DataFrame({'region_code': ['NORTH', 'SOUTH'], 'meter_count': [125000, 140000]})
spark_df = spark.createDataFrame(pd_df)

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Converting from Pandas collects ALL data onto the driver. This works for small reference tables but will crash the driver if the Pandas DataFrame is large. Never use this pattern for production data.
Every DataFrame has a schema: a list of column names and their data types. The schema is the contract between your code and the data.
Inferred Schema

df = spark.read.csv('meters.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
df.printSchema()
Notice that date was inferred as string, not DateType. Spark saw values like '2026-01-15' and had no way to know they represent dates.
Explicit Schema

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, DateType

meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType(), nullable=False),
    StructField('daily_kw', DoubleType(), nullable=True),
    StructField('date',     DateType(),   nullable=False),
    StructField('region',   StringType(), nullable=False),
])

df = spark.read.csv('meters.csv', header=True, schema=meter_schema)
The StructType Building Blocks

blocks.title: Production rule
blocks.content: Always use explicit schemas in production pipelines. inferSchema is for exploration only. An explicit schema is faster, safer, and documents itself.
Every DataFrame operation returns a new DataFrame. The original is never modified. This is the foundation of safe distributed processing.

raw = spark.read.csv('meters.csv', header=True, schema=meter_schema)
filtered = raw.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
enriched = filtered.withColumn('daily_mw', col('daily_kw') / 1000)

# All three DataFrames exist independently
raw.count()       # all rows
filtered.count()  # only positive
enriched.count()  # same as filtered, extra column
The Reassignment Pattern

df = spark.read.csv('meters.csv', header=True, schema=meter_schema)
df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
df = df.withColumn('daily_mw', col('daily_kw') / 1000)
df = df.drop('daily_kw')
# Each line creates a new DataFrame. 'df' just points to the latest one.

blocks.title: Why it matters
blocks.content: Immutability means you can always go back. If a transformation produces unexpected results, the input DataFrame is untouched. This makes debugging straightforward.
Spark does not execute transformations when you write them. It records them into a plan. Only when you call an action — show(), count(), write() — does Spark optimize and execute.
Building the Plan

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year

df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')           # transformation
df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)                 # transformation
df = df.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')            # transformation
df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))        # transformation
df = df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'year')       # transformation

df.show(5)  # THIS triggers execution
What Catalyst Optimizes
Catalyst pushes filters into the reader, combines filters, and reads only selected columns. You wrote five steps; Spark might execute two.
Transformations vs Actions

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Calling .count() inside a loop triggers a full execution each time. Use .show(5) during development. Save .count() for validation checkpoints.
A Column in PySpark is not a list of values. It is an expression — a description of a computation that will be applied to every row when an action triggers execution.
Four Ways to Reference a Column

from pyspark.sql.functions import col

df.select('daily_kw')              # string name
df.select(col('daily_kw'))         # col() function — recommended
df.select(df.daily_kw)             # dot notation
df.select(df['daily_kw'])          # bracket notation
Columns Are Expressions

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when

expr = col('daily_kw') / 1000
tier = when(col('daily_kw') < 500, 'Low') \
       .when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, 'Medium') \
       .otherwise('High')

df = df.withColumn('daily_mw', expr)
df = df.withColumn('usage_tier', tier)

blocks.title: Key insight
blocks.content: Think of Column objects as formulas in a spreadsheet cell. The formula =A1/1000 is not a number — it is an instruction. PySpark columns work the same way.
Raw data rarely arrives with clean column names. Spark can handle messy names, but downstream code is cleaner with snake_case, no spaces, lowercase.
Renaming with alias() in select()

df = df.select(
    col('Meter ID').alias('meter_id'),
    col('Daily kW').alias('daily_kw'),
    col('Reading Date').alias('date'),
    col('Region')  # no rename needed
)
Dropping Columns
df = df.drop('raw_timestamp', 'source_file', 'batch_id')

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Use select() to whitelist columns. Use drop() to remove one or two extras. Whitelisting is safer because it fails loudly if a column is missing.
PySpark's immutability creates a natural coding pattern: method chaining. Each transformation returns a new DataFrame, so you can stack calls into a readable pipeline.
The Chain Pattern

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year, upper, when

cleaned = (
    spark.read.csv('bronze/meters.csv', header=True, schema=meter_schema)
    .filter(col('daily_kw').isNotNull())
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
    .filter(col('daily_kw') < 50000)
    .withColumn('region', upper(col('region')))
    .withColumn('daily_mw', col('daily_kw') / 1000)
    .withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
    .withColumn('usage_tier',
        when(col('daily_kw') < 500, 'Low')
        .when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, 'Medium')
        .otherwise('High')
    )
    .select('meter_id', 'date', 'region', 'daily_kw', 'daily_mw', 'year', 'usage_tier')
)

blocks.title: Readability rule
blocks.content: If another engineer cannot understand your chain in 30 seconds, break it up. Name intermediates after what they represent: raw, validated, enriched, aggregated.

printSchema() is the single most underused tool in PySpark development. It shows every column name, type, and nullable flag. Reading the schema before writing transformations prevents entire categories of bugs.
What Nullable Means
nullable = true means the column can contain null values. This matters for joins (nulls never match), aggregations (nulls are skipped), and filters (null comparisons return null, not true or false).
Programmatic Schema Access

print(df.columns)         # ['meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'date', ...]
print(df.dtypes)          # [('meter_id', 'string'), ('daily_kw', 'double'), ...]

if 'daily_kw' in df.columns:
    df = df.withColumn('daily_mw', col('daily_kw') / 1000)

blocks.title: Production habit
blocks.content: Run printSchema() at the start of every notebook or pipeline step. It takes zero time (schemas are metadata) and prevents the most common errors: wrong column names, wrong types, unexpected nulls.

Every transformation adds a node to an invisible structure called the DAG. The DAG is Spark's complete recipe from source data to final output. Understanding it explains how Spark plans execution, recovers from failures, and optimizes your code.
Stages and Tasks

Catalyst compiles the DAG into stages. Narrow transformations like filter() stay in the same stage. Wide transformations like groupBy() force a stage boundary because data must shuffle. Within each stage, one task per partition runs in parallel.
Lineage and Fault Tolerance

If an executor crashes and a partition is lost, Spark looks at the DAG, identifies which source partitions fed the lost partition, and recomputes just that slice. This is lineage-based fault tolerance.

blocks.title: Key insight
blocks.content: The DAG is why immutability and lazy evaluation exist. Immutability means every transformation is a pure function. Laziness means Spark builds the complete DAG before executing. Together, they let Spark optimize, parallelize, and recover from any failure.

chapters.title: Join Performance and Shuffle Optimization
chapters.description: Understand why joins are the most performance-sensitive operation in Spark, master broadcast and shuffle join strategies, detect and resolve data skew.

blocks.content: In the junior-level track, you learned that joins combine two DataFrames on a matching key. Now we look at the cost — and the answer is: more than any other operation. A join that works on 100,000 rows can bring a cluster to its knees at 100 million rows, not because the logic is wrong but because the physical execution strategy is wrong.
The Shuffle Problem

When you write readings.join(metadata, 'meter_id'), Spark must ensure that every row with the same meter_id ends up on the same executor. This movement of data across the network is called a shuffle, and it is the single most expensive operation in distributed computing.

result = readings.join(metadata, 'meter_id')
result.explain()

== Physical Plan ==
*(5) SortMergeJoin [meter_id#0], [meter_id#10], Inner
:- *(2) Sort [meter_id#0 ASC]
:  +- Exchange hashpartitioning(meter_id#0, 200)    ← SHUFFLE
+- *(4) Sort [meter_id#10 ASC]
   +- Exchange hashpartitioning(meter_id#10, 200)    ← SHUFFLE

blocks.title: Key insight
blocks.content: The cost of a join is not determined by the complexity of your join condition. It is determined by how much data must move across the network.

What if instead of shuffling both sides, we sent the entire metadata table (50MB) to every executor? Each executor would have a complete local copy and could join locally. This is a broadcast join — the single most impactful performance optimization for joins where one side is small.

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

result = readings.join(broadcast(metadata), 'meter_id')
result.explain()

== Physical Plan ==
*(2) BroadcastHashJoin [meter_id#0], [meter_id#10], Inner
:- *(1) FileScan parquet
+- BroadcastExchange HashedRelationBroadcastMode
   +- FileScan parquet
No 'Exchange hashpartitioning' — no shuffle. Network I/O reduced from 2.05GB to 150MB — a 13x improvement.
When to Broadcast

rows: Automatic. Spark broadcasts without any hint.
Never broadcast. Use shuffle join or bucketed join.

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Raising autoBroadcastJoinThreshold to 2GB to 'always use broadcast.' This causes OutOfMemoryError when tables are actually large.
When broadcast is not an option, Spark falls back to SortMergeJoin. This is the workhorse for production pipelines where both DataFrames contain millions or billions of rows.
The Three Phases
Phase 1: Shuffle both sides by join key. Phase 2: Sort each partition. Phase 3: Merge-walk both sorted partitions linearly. The merge is O(n) and cheap. The shuffle and sort are where cost lives.

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Always check the physical plan. Run result.explain() and look for 'Exchange' lines. Each Exchange is a shuffle.

Your pipeline ran fine for six months. Then it started taking 4 hours. One region grew from 25% to 60% of all readings. The shuffle sends 60% of data to one executor while others sit idle. This is data skew.
readings.groupBy('region').count().orderBy('count', ascending=False).show()
Three Strategies for Skew Resolution
Salting splits the hot key into sub-keys. AQE detects and fixes skew automatically. Pre-aggregation reduces volume before the join.

# Strategy 1: Salting
SALT_BUCKETS = 10
readings_salted = readings.withColumn(
    'salt', floor(rand() * SALT_BUCKETS).cast('int')
).withColumn(
    'meter_id_salted', concat(col('meter_id'), lit('_'), col('salt'))
)

blocks.title: Salting trade-off
blocks.content: Salting multiplies the small side by the number of buckets. A 50MB table becomes 500MB with 10 buckets.
spark.sql.shuffle.partitions defaults to 200. This single parameter affects every groupBy, join, distinct, and window function. Target 128-256MB per partition.

# Calculate optimal partitions
total_gb = 2.0
target_mb = 200
optimal = int((total_gb * 1024) / target_mb)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', str(optimal))
Adaptive Query Execution

spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '2000')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes', '200m')

blocks.title: Production rule
blocks.content: On Spark 3.2+, enable AQE and set shuffle.partitions high (2000-4000). Let AQE handle coalescing.

Catalyst rearranges your operations automatically — pushing filters toward the data source even if you wrote them after the join. Understanding what Catalyst will and will not optimize helps you cooperate with the optimizer.

# You write filter AFTER join — Catalyst pushes it down automatically
result = readings.join(metadata, 'meter_id').filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')
Catalyst cannot push filters through UDFs, cross-table conditions, or post-aggregation filters. In those cases, manually restructure your code.

blocks.title: Key insight
blocks.content: Trust Catalyst for simple column filters. Do not trust it for UDFs or cross-table conditions. Manually restructure for those cases.
Bucketing pays the shuffle cost once at write time so every subsequent join is free. Both tables must use the same number of buckets and the same column.

readings.write.mode('overwrite').bucketBy(200, 'meter_id').sortBy('meter_id').saveAsTable('silver.readings_bucketed')
metadata.write.mode('overwrite').bucketBy(200, 'meter_id').sortBy('meter_id').saveAsTable('silver.metadata_bucketed')

# Join is now shuffle-free
result = spark.table('silver.readings_bucketed').join(spark.table('silver.metadata_bucketed'), 'meter_id')

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Both tables MUST use the same number of buckets. If readings uses 200 and metadata uses 100, Spark falls back to shuffle join.
You now know four strategies: broadcast, sort-merge, bucketed, and salting. The question is which to choose for each specific join.
Strategy Summary

rows: Both large, joined repeatedly on same key

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: For NordGrid daily: broadcast metadata (50MB), default sort-merge for readings-SCADA, enable AQE for skew. For yearly reprocessing: bucket both sides.

AQE optimizes during execution based on actual observed data. It provides three critical optimizations: converting sort-merge to broadcast at runtime, coalescing small partitions, and splitting skewed partitions.

spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor', '5')

blocks.title: Key insight
blocks.content: AQE is not a replacement for understanding join performance — it is a safety net. Enable it everywhere. But when you see unexpected slowness, diagnose the join strategy yourself.
Knowing the right strategy is necessary but not sufficient. You need to detect join performance degradation before it causes an SLA breach.
Key Metrics
Join Health Check Function

def join_health_check(left_df, right_df, join_key):
    left_count = left_df.count()
    right_count = right_df.count()
    left_keys = left_df.select(countDistinct(join_key)).collect()[0][0]
    key_dist = left_df.groupBy(join_key).count()
    stats = key_dist.agg(spark_max('count'), spark_min('count')).collect()[0]
    skew_ratio = stats[0] / max(stats[1], 1)
    print(f'Skew ratio: {skew_ratio:.1f}x')
    if skew_ratio > 10:
        print('WARNING: Significant skew detected')

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Add a join health check to every pipeline with an SLA. It costs one extra pass but catches problems that would otherwise cost hours of debugging.

chapters.title: Partition Strategy at Scale
chapters.description: Design, implement, and maintain partition strategies for multi-terabyte datasets.

blocks.content: You know how partitioning works. You used partitionBy('region', 'date') and it was straightforward: 4 regions × 365 days = 1,460 partitions per year. GridUnion Continental operates 30 regions across 4 countries with 5 million smart meters. The same strategy produces 262,800 partitions per year at hourly granularity. The file system strains, the metastore becomes a bottleneck, and partition discovery takes minutes.
What This Module Covers

This module is about designing partition strategies that work at scale and survive production for years. We cover partition column selection, granularity trade-offs, dynamic partition pruning, small file remediation, and repartitioning live tables.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: A partition strategy is not a performance optimization — it is a data architecture decision with a 2-3 year lifespan. Changing it after deployment requires rewriting terabytes. Get it right at design time.

The partition column determines how data is physically organized on disk. Every query that filters on it skips irrelevant data at the file system level. This makes partition column selection the highest-leverage decision in table design.
Selection Criteria

The ideal partition column satisfies four criteria: appears in WHERE clauses of frequent queries, has moderate cardinality (30-500 values), distributes data roughly evenly, and is immutable after write.
GridUnion's Meter Readings: The Decision
Modeling Partition Sizes

blocks.title: Design principle
blocks.content: Always model partition sizes before implementing. Target 128MB-1GB per partition. Run the model for current volumes and projected volumes 2 years out.
Finer granularity means better pruning but more partitions. Coarser means fewer partitions but more data scanned. The right granularity depends on query patterns and data volume.
The Granularity Spectrum

blocks.title: Architectural trap
blocks.content: Do not let ingestion cadence determine partition granularity. Data arriving hourly does not require hourly partitions. Granularity is determined by query patterns and partition size targets.
Dynamic partition pruning (DPP) extends static pruning to join predicates, where pruning values come from another query at runtime. DPP is the most impactful optimization for star schema queries.

# Star schema query — DPP optimizes automatically
result = (
    fact_readings
    .join(dim_regions, 'region_id')
    .filter(col('country') == 'DE')
    .groupBy('date')
    .agg(F.sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw'))
)
# With DPP: Spark reads dim_regions, gets DE region_ids, prunes fact partitions
# Without DPP: Spark reads ALL partitions then filters after join

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: Design partition columns to align with star schema join paths. Partition strategy is not just a storage decision — it is a query optimization decision designed from the consumer backward.

Every partition strategy eventually produces small files. A 1MB file and a 256MB file take almost the same time to open. When Spark reads 10,000 small files, it spends more time opening files than reading data.
Delta Lake OPTIMIZE

# Delta Lake compaction — one command
spark.sql("OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings WHERE country = 'DE' AND date = '2026-01-15'")

# Z-ORDER for multi-column optimization
spark.sql("OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings WHERE country = 'DE' ZORDER BY (meter_id)")

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: OPTIMIZE runs nightly on previous day's partitions. Budget 15-30 minutes per TB. This is non-negotiable operational cost of partitioning.
Small tables, lookup tables, dimension tables, and tables with unpredictable query patterns are all worse off with partitioning.
Decision Framework

blocks.title: Anti-pattern: over-partitioning
blocks.content: A platform audit found 40% of tables were partitioned unnecessarily — including tables under 100MB. Cleanup removed 200,000 empty partition directories and reduced catalog listing time by 60%.
Partitioning organizes data into directories. Z-ORDER organizes data within files. Where partitioning answers 'which directory?', Z-ORDER answers 'which row groups within this file can I skip?'
Z-ORDER works best on high-cardinality columns frequently filtered but not suitable as partition columns. meter_id is canonical: 5M distinct values, frequently filtered, highly selective.

spark.sql("""
    OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings
    WHERE country = 'DE' AND date >= '2026-01-01'
    ZORDER BY (meter_id)
""")

blocks.title: Two-tier optimization
blocks.content: Partitioning is coarse-grained (directory level). Z-ORDER is fine-grained (within-file level). Use partitioning for low-cardinality filters. Use Z-ORDER for high-cardinality filters. Together they form a two-tier optimization.

GridUnion's meter readings table was partitioned by date only. Regulatory requirements now mandate country-level isolation. The table holds 8TB. 12 pipelines and 40 dashboards depend on it. Zero downtime required.
Three Approaches

# Delta Lake CRAS — atomic repartitioning
spark.sql("""
    CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE silver.meter_readings
    USING DELTA
    PARTITIONED BY (country, date)
    AS SELECT *, CASE WHEN region LIKE 'DE-%' THEN 'DE' END AS country
    FROM silver.meter_readings
""")

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The best repartitioning is the one you never need. Invest time upfront to model for 2-year volumes. If unavoidable, Delta CRAS is lowest-risk.
Without governance, each of 12 teams chooses its own strategy. Cross-country queries become nightmarish because each table has different partition columns and granularity.
The Partition Standard

blocks.title: Governance pattern
blocks.content: Publish the partition standard as machine-readable YAML — not a wiki page. CI/CD hooks can validate automatically. Wiki pages drift from reality within months.

Every new table >10GB must go through a partition strategy review. The review ensures the design accounts for current and projected volumes, query patterns, consumer needs, and compaction obligations.
The Review Template

rows: Name, catalog location, owner team, category
Columns, modeled partition count and average size
Top 5 queries, which columns in WHERE clauses
What if volume doubles? Repartitioning fallback?

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The partition strategy review is the cheapest point to catch design errors. A 30-minute review saves a 6-hour repartitioning, a 2-week migration, and the inevitable 3 AM incident.
